The book called, Fineite, Nymphenic Games, in which he defined these two kinds of games.
Fineite games, which have known players, fixed rules, and agreed upon objectives, total.
There's always a beginning, a middle and an end, and the objective is to win the game.
And then there's infinite games.
Infinite games are defined as known and unknown players.
You don't necessarily know who all the other players are.
The rules are changeable.
You can play however you want.
And the objective is to perpetuate the game to stay in the game as long as possible.
What I find so fascinating about this idea when I first learned about it is we are players and multiple infinite games every day of our lives.

There's no such thing as winning global politics, and there's definitely no such thing as winning business.

And so what ends up happening is you have people building organizations in leading with a finite mindset, playing to win in a game where there's no such thing as winning.

A monkswiction include the decline of trust, the decline of cooperation, the decline of innovation, all of which contribute to the eventual demise of the organization itself.
And so what I wrote about is what it means to lead with an infinite mindset because we teach leadership as if it were a finite game.

There's nothing wrong with revenues.
There are two currencies in the infinite game, will and resources. Resources as money, you need money to stay in the game, but you need the will of the people as well.

into any number of presentations where they put their priorities on the wall, number one growth, number two shareholder value, number three, our customer number four, our employees, there it is.

And the reason I wrote the infinite game was because I myself got tired of being told by people in positions of authority or people of extreme wealth that I was naive that I don't understand how business works.

we don't care how you get there. We don't care if you step on your people. We don't care if you act unethically.
